description: Review key concepts in special senses physiology.

cards.text: What is the difference between conductive and sensorineural deafness?
cards.answer: Conductive: problem in external or middle ear (wax, otitis media, ossicle damage). Sensorineural: damage to cochlea or CN VIII (noise exposure, presbycusis, ototoxic drugs). Weber lateralises to affected ear in conductive, away in sensorineural. Rinne negative in conductive.

tip.tip: Think about where the sound pathway is blocked.

cards.text: What is the visual pathway from retina to cortex?
cards.answer: Retina (ganglion cells) -> optic nerve -> optic chiasma (nasal fibres cross) -> optic tract -> lateral geniculate body (thalamus) -> optic radiation -> primary visual cortex (area 17, calcarine sulcus).

tip.tip: Nasal fibres cross at the chiasma.

cards.text: What are the five primary taste modalities?
cards.answer: Sweet, salty, sour, bitter, and umami. Each has specific receptor mechanisms: salty and sour use ion channels; sweet, bitter, and umami use G-protein coupled receptors.

tip.tip: Umami was the most recently identified taste.

cards.text: How does the pupillary light reflex work?
cards.answer: Light -> retina -> optic nerve -> pretectal nucleus -> bilateral Edinger-Westphal nuclei -> CN III -> ciliary ganglion -> constrictor pupillae. Both pupils constrict (consensual response) because pretectal nucleus projects bilaterally.

tip.tip: Afferent via CN II, efferent via CN III.

cards.text: What is presbyopia and why does it occur?
cards.answer: Age-related loss of near vision (accommodation). The lens loses elasticity with age, reducing its ability to increase curvature for near focus. Typically begins around age 40. Corrected with convex (plus) lenses.

Otoconia (calcium carbonate crystals) from the utricle dislodge into the semicircular canals (usually posterior). Head movement causes inappropriate stimulation of hair cells, creating a false sense of rotation. Treated with Epley manoeuvre.

The process by which the eye adjusts from bright to dim light. Cones adapt quickly (7-8 min) but have limited sensitivity. Rods take 30-40 minutes to fully adapt by regenerating rhodopsin, achieving much greater sensitivity.

tip.tip: Rods take longer but achieve greater sensitivity.

cards.answer: Olfactory receptors in nasal mucosa -> olfactory nerve (CN I) through cribriform plate -> olfactory bulb -> olfactory tract -> primary olfactory cortex (piriform cortex). Unique: does not relay through thalamus before reaching cortex.

tip.tip: The only sense that bypasses the thalamus.
